coredata.dc:title: In vitro genotoxicity assessment of graphene quantum dots nanoparticles: A metabolism-dependent response
coredata.prism:publicationName: Mutation Research/Genetic Toxicology and Environmental Mutagenesis
coredata.dc:creator: Goldstein, Alana da Cunha
Araujo-Lima, Carlos Fernando
Fernandes, Andreia da Silva
Santos-Oliveira, Ralph
Felzenszwalb, Israel
coredata.dc:description: Nanomaterials are progressively being applied in different areas, including biomedical uses. Carbon nanomaterials are relevant for biomedical sciences because of their biocompatibility properties. Graphene quantum dots (GQD) have a substantial potential in drug-delivery nanostructured biosystems, but there is still a lack of toxicological information regarding their effects on human health and the environment. We thus evaluated the mutagenicity, cytotoxicity and genotoxicity of this nanomaterial using alternative methods applied in regulatory toxicology guidelines. The Ames test was carried out in the presence and absence of exogenous metabolization. Salmonella enterica serovar Typhimurium strains TA97a, TA98, TA100, TA102, TA104, and TA1535 were exposed to GQD with concentrations ranging from 1 to 1000 μg/plate. The mammal cell viability assays were carried out with HepG2 and 3T3BalbC cell lineages and the in vitro Cytokinesis-Block Micronucleus assay (CBMN) was applied for 24 h of exposure in non-cytotoxic concentrations. Mutagenicity was induced in the TA97a strain in the absence of exogenous metabolization, but not in its presence. Mutagenicity was also detected in the TA102 strain in the assay with exogenous metabolization, suggesting redox misbalance mutagenicity. The WST-1 and LDH assays demonstrated that GQD decreased cell viability, especially in 3T3BalbC cells, which showed more sensitivity to the nanomaterial. GQD also increased micronuclei formation in 3T3BalbC and caused a cytostatic effect. No significant impact on HepG2 micronuclei formation was observed. Different metabolic systems interfered with the mutagenic, cytotoxic, and genotoxic effects of GQD, indicating that liver metabolism has a central role in the detoxification of this nanomaterial.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Nanotoxicology
Mutagenicity
Cytotoxicity
Genotoxicity

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to thank the financial support by the Coordination of Superior Level Staff Improvement ( CAPES ) (Code 0001 ); The Carlos Chagas Filho Foundation for Research Support of the State of Rio de Janeiro ( FAPERJ ), awards: E-26/200.535/2020 (ACG), E-26/202.759/2017 (IF), E26/202.256/2018 (ASF), E-26/010.002119/2019 (CFA-L and IF), E-26/010.100956/2018 (CFA-L) and E-26/010.000981/2019 (CFA-L, RS-O and IF); and The National Council for Scientific and Technological Development ( CNPq ), awards: 302345/2017-5 (IF).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para:
Revertant colonies of the TA98 (A-E), TA100 (B-F), TA102 (C-G), and TA104 (D-H) strains of Salmonella enterica serovar Typhimurium His
+ in the Ames test, after incubation with graphene quantum dots (GQD) in the absence (-S9) and presence (+S9) of the S9 mix at 4%. The positive controls (P.C.) in the -S9 assays were 4-nitroquinoline-N-oxide (4NQO) (1.0 µg/plate) for TA98, sodium azide (1.0 µg/plate) for TA100, mytomicin-C (0.5 µg/plate) for TA102, and methanesulphonate (250 µg/plate) for TA104. The controls in presence of exogenous metabolization were 2-aminoanthracene (2-AA) (100 µg/plate) for TA98 and TA100 and benzo-α-pyrene (BaP) (20 µg/plate) for TA102 and TA104. #, data published in De [22]; *, Difference from the negative control; ¢, cytotoxic; one-way ANOVA followed by Tukey's post hoc test (p < 0.05).

Revertant colonies of the TA97a (A and B) and TA1535 (C and D) strains of Salmonella enterica serovar Typhimurium His
+ in the Ames test, after incubation with graphene quantum dots (GQD) in the absence (-S9) and presence (+S9) of the S9 mix at 4%. The positive controls (P.C.) in the -S9 tests were 4-nitroquinoline 1-oxide (4NQO; 2 µg/plate) for TA97a and sodium azide (0.5 µg/plate) for TA1535. In the +S9 assays, 2-aminoanthracene (2-AA) was used for both the TA97a (5 µg/plate) and TA1535 (20 µg/plate) strains. *, Difference from the negative control; ¢, cytotoxic; one-way ANOVA followed by Tukey's post hoc test (p < 0.05).

Cytotoxicity of Graphene Quantum Dots (GQD). 3T3 BalbC (A-C) and HepG2 (D-F) cells were incubated with GQDs for 24 h, 48 h and 72 h. After the incubations, cell membrane damage (LDH assay) and mitochondrial function (WST-1 assay) were measured. The 50% lethal concentrations of the cell population (LC50) (G) values were calculated from the nonlinear regression fit of the dose–response.

Graphene Quantum Dots (GQD) micronuclei induction in 3T3BalbC and HepG2 cells.. To determinate the number of cells with micronuclei (MN) 3000 binucleated (BNC) cells were scored per treatment for each experiment. The Nuclear Division Index (NDI) was calculated by counting another 1000 cells, including mononucleated, binucleated and multinucleated cells per coverslip. MMS: methyl methanesulfonate, 50 μM; BaP: Benzo-α-pyrene, 50 μg/mL(∗p < 0.05; ∗∗p < 0.01; ∗∗∗p < 0.001 versus the negative control; n = 3 in triplicate; one-way ANOVA followed by a Tukey’s post hoc test).

head.ce:title: In vitro genotoxicity assessment of graphene quantum dots nanoparticles: A metabolism-dependent response

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally in this study.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nanomaterials are progressively being applied in different areas, including biomedical uses. Carbon nanomaterials are relevant for biomedical sciences because of their biocompatibility properties. Graphene quantum dots (GQD) have a substantial potential in drug-delivery nanostructured biosystems, but there is still a lack of toxicological information regarding their effects on human health and the environment. We thus evaluated the mutagenicity, cytotoxicity and genotoxicity of this nanomaterial using alternative methods applied in regulatory toxicology guidelines. The Ames test was carried out in the presence and absence of exogenous metabolization. Salmonella enterica serovar Typhimurium strains TA97a, TA98, TA100, TA102, TA104, and TA1535 were exposed to GQD with concentrations ranging from 1 to 1000 μg/plate. The mammal cell viability assays were carried out with HepG2 and 3T3BalbC cell lineages and the in vitro Cytokinesis-Block Micronucleus assay (CBMN) was applied for 24 h of exposure in non-cytotoxic concentrations. Mutagenicity was induced in the TA97a strain in the absence of exogenous metabolization, but not in its presence. Mutagenicity was also detected in the TA102 strain in the assay with exogenous metabolization, suggesting redox misbalance mutagenicity. The WST-1 and LDH assays demonstrated that GQD decreased cell viability, especially in 3T3BalbC cells, which showed more sensitivity to the nanomaterial. GQD also increased micronuclei formation in 3T3BalbC and caused a cytostatic effect. No significant impact on HepG2 micronuclei formation was observed. Different metabolic systems interfered with the mutagenic, cytotoxic, and genotoxic effects of GQD, indicating that liver metabolism has a central role in the detoxification of this nanomaterial.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanomaterials are substances with sizes ranging from 1 to 100 nanometers. They present applications in a variety of fields such as electronics, paints, clothing, agriculture, and medicine, leading to improvements in these areas, and there has been a rise in the use, marketing, and commercialization of nanomaterials in recent years [41].

There are several uses for nanomaterials in biomedical sciences, such as the treatment of different disorders, ranging from infections to neurodegenerative diseases and cancer [12]. Besides their ability to reach never-before-reached places, their size provides these materials with unusual physicochemical properties [13], the main feature being their greater surface contact since this allows for more interactions with the environment and organisms [36].

Carbon-based nanomaterials display various conformations, such as single walled nanotubes, multiwalled nanotubes, fullerene carbon black and graphene. These different physicochemical properties result in distinct effects regarding the environment and its biota [44]. Probable applications include electronics, wastewater treatment [30], bioimaging, tissue engineering and drug delivery [24]. However, there is evidence of bioaccumulation which may injure the organisms present [9].

Chemical stability, fluorescence and biocompatibility make graphene quantum dots (GQD) promising candidates, especially for biomedical purposes [16]. Their sp² hybridized carbons enable drug-carrier ability; their fluorescence allows for application in imaging and shows the potential to act as a drug on its own [17,19].

As more nanomaterials are applied in every-day life, concern with possible effects on humans and the environment have arisen [21], and it is the responsibility of toxicological assessment to authenticate the safety of substances by evaluating the risks to organisms presented by the chemicals under test [31].

The water-soluble tetrazolium salt assay (WST-1) is applied to evaluate cytotoxicity via the mitochondrial dehydrogenase activity. When active, these enzymes can modify the tetrazolium salt, shifting the colour of the medium from red to yellow, allowing for the indirect assessment of mitochondrial activity via absorbance of the culture medium. The lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) assay is carried out for further cytotoxicity evaluation. The membrane releases lactate dehydrogenase, an intracellular enzyme, into the culture medium when damaged, and this enzyme uses the tetrazolium salt as its substrate, allowing for a colorimetric analysis. Thus, an increase in LDH activity in the medium indicates enhanced cytotoxicity in the cells analysed. The cytokinesis-block micronucleus (CBMN) assay [23,28] can survey the aneugenic or clastogenic potential of test substances. The effects of genotoxic agents in the chromosomes are made evident by the presence of micronuclei in dividing cells. As cytochalasin B inhibits cytokinesis in mitotic cells, it is implemented to allow for visualization of micronuclei in binucleated cells.

GQD showed a mutagenic response in the Ames test without S9; so it was only evaluated in the presence of exogenous metabolization [22]. Thus, the aim of this study was to assess the mutagenic, cytotoxic and genotoxic aspects of the toxicity of GQDs using in vitro methods, following the guidelines of agencies on regulatory toxicology for chemical items intended for use in improving human and animal health [1,7].

Graphene Quantum Dots (QGD) were produced and characterized according to the description previously published by our group in De [22]. In summary, powder X-ray diffraction showed a graphene peak at 2θ = 10 and a Raman analysis confirmed the presence of GQD since it detected the presence of bands at ∼1360 cm-1 and ∼1600 cm-1. Dynamic light scattering determined the average size of the GQD as 167.3 nm. Atomic force microscopy placed the diameter in the interval of 160–280 nm, with a medium height of 5 nm. The sample was then diluted in water with the aid of a vortex mixer and stored at − 20 ºC until used.

Mutagenicity was evaluated using the Ames pre-incubation test according to OECD Test Guideline No 471 [27]. The sample tested is considered mutagenic when the number of revertant colonies is at least twice that presented by the negative control, showing a clear dose-response curve or statistically different when compared to the negative control.

Rat liver homogenate (4% S9 mix, Aroclor 1254 induced, from Moltox Inc., USA) was used to mimic the metabolization of mammal phase I liver metabolism. Its use is recommended since the metabolites may impose more hazards than their original counterparts [25].

Screening for the phenotypical profile of the strains from our laboratory frozen stock was carried out before the procedure. Strains TA97a, TA98, TA100, TA102, TA104, and TA1535 were maintained in a shaker at 210 rpm overnight. Aliquots of 100 μL of inoculum (1–2×108 cells) and 500 μL of the S9 fraction were added to the test tubes, or 500 μL of sodium phosphate buffer (0.2 M, pH 7.4) in the TA1535 and TA97a -S9 assay, and 100 μL of GQD diluted in sterile water (0.1, 1, 10, 100, or 1000 μg/plate) or 100 μL of controls, then incorporated in petri dishes containing Vogel-Bonner agar medium. The negative control used was sterile water while the positive controls in the presence of exogenous metabolization were 2-aminoanthracene (2-AA) for TA98, TA100 (100 µg/plate), TA97a (5 µg/plate), and TA1535 (20 µg/plate) and Benzo-α-pyrene (BaP; 20 µg/plate) for TA102 and TA104. In the absence of exogenous metabolization the positive control was 4-nitroquinoline 1-oxide (4NQO) for TA97a (2 µg/plate) and TA98 (1.0 µg/plate), sodium azide for TA100 (1.0 µg/plate) and TA1535 (0.5 µg/plate), mytomicin-C for TA102 (0.5 µg/plate) and methanesulphonate for TA104 (250 µg/plate). After 20 min of incubation with agitation, 2 mL of melted top agar were added to the test tubes. The mixture was plated in petri dishes containing Vogel-Bonner agar and the revertant colonies counted after 72 h of incubation at 37ºC [25]. According to the OECD, Guidelines 471 [27], the mutagenic response of GQD is considered positive when the number of revertant colonies in the test is at least twice the number of spontaneous revertant colonies, and when a significant response to the analysis of variance (ANOVA) (p ≤ 0.05) followed by Tukey’s post hoc test and a reproducible positive dose-response curve are found. The tests were carried out in triplicate and repeated at least twice. The results were calculated as the mean plus the standard error values.

Cell viability assays were carried out using mouse fibroblast (3T3BalbC) and human hepatocarcinoma (HepG2) cell lines. The 3T3BalbC line was purchased from the Banco de Células do Rio de Janeiro (BCRJ) and the HepG2 cells were kindly provided by Prof. Danielle Palma de Oliveira of the University of São Paulo, Brazil. Both cell lines were cultured in Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM) with 1% penicillin-streptomycin (10,000 U:10 mg/mL). For HepG2, 10% heat-inactivated foetal bovine serum was also included and for 3T3BalbC, 10% heat-inactivated calf serum. The cells were maintained in culture flasks at 37ºC, 95% humidity and 5% CO2 until 80–90% confluence was reached [10].

The cells were seeded in a 96 well-plate with 1×104 cells/well and a final volume of 100 μL. After incubation for 24 h at 37 ºC and 5% CO2, the cells were treated with GQD (up to 1000 μg/mL, on a semi log scale), the negative control (distilled water) or the positive control (5% Triton X-100) for 24, 48, and 72 h. After the designated time, 90 μL of fresh medium and 10 μL of the WST reagent were added to each well, followed by 3 h of incubation at 37 ºC and 5% CO2, and the plate finally read at 450 nm in the absence of light (Polaris Microplate Reader, Celer, Brazil) [10,32].

The cells were seeded in a 96 well-plate at 1×104 cells/well and incubated for 24 h at 37 ºC and 5% CO2. The cells received the same treatment as in the WST assay. After the treatment period, the supernatant was moved to a new 96-well plate and the LDH reagent added. The plate was read at 492 nm (Polaris Microplate Reader, Celer, Brazil) after 30 min of incubation at room temperature protected from the light [10,32]. The percentage of cytotoxicity was calculated according to the kit protocols. The lethal concentration (LC50) for 50% of cultured cells was determined by comparison based on this reference level. The results for LC50 represent the average ± SD of three independent experiments for each cell viability assay. To evaluate the possible impact of GQD fluorescence in both assays, a background check was carried out with the medium and GQD (data not shown). No difference in the absorbance profiles was observed, indicating no interference in the optical density measurements.

The cytokinesis-block micronucleus (CBMN) assay [23,28] was carried out to survey the aneugenic or clastogenic potential of the GQDs on 3T3BalbC and HepG2 cells. The effects of genotoxic agents on the chromosomes are made evident by the presence of micronuclei in dividing cells. Since cytochalasin B (cytB) inhibits cytokinesis in mitotic cells, it was implemented to allow for the visualization of micronuclei in binucleated cells.

The 3T3BalbC cells were seeded in 24-well plates with 5×104 cells/well and the HepG2 with 2–3×104 cells/well, using previously sterilized 13 mm circular coverslips. After an overnight adherence period, the cells were exposed for 24 h either to GQD (0.1, 1, 10, 100, or 1000 μg/mL), or to the negative (distilled water) or positive (methyl methanesulfonate, MMS at 50 μM for 3T3BalbC cells or BAP at 50 μg/mL for HepG2 cells) controls at 37ºC and 5% CO2. Considering the doubling time of the cells (20 ± 4 h), the supernatant was then discarded and replaced by 1 mL of fresh medium with cytB (3 μg/mL) added and incubated for another 20 h. The cells were then washed once with PBS, fixed with 1 mL of a methanol and acetic acid solution (3:1) for 1 h, and stained using diluted Giemsa (1:10) for 1 h. For each concentration the slides were prepared with Entellan.

To determine the number of micronucleated cells and other cytogenetic damage events, such as nucleoplasmic bridges and nuclear buds, 1000 binucleated cells were counted (a total of 3000 binucleated cells per concentration) and the Nuclear Division Index (NDI) calculated by counting another 1000 cells, including mononucleated, binucleated and multinucleated cells per coverslip, analysed under a light microscope, in each of at least three independent experiments, after coding the slides to reduce analytical bias [2].

After counting the cells, the NDI was used as a parameter to investigate any possible cytostatic and cytotoxic actions.
NDI = [(1xN1) + (2xN2) + (3xN3)] /N
N1 = Mononucleated cells; N2 = Binucleated cells; N3 = Multinucleated cells; N = total number of cells analysed.

The one-way ANOVA test followed by the Tukey's post-hoc test were applied using the GraphPad Prism software version 5 (GraphPad Software, Inc.) for the mutagenicity and genotoxicity assays. The results were considered significant when p < 0.05. The results of the cytotoxicity assays were analysed using the Microsoft Excel software, where the nonlinear regression fit of the dose–response (agonist behaviour) curves was calculated.

In the presence of exogenous metabolization, GQD induced mutagenicity at 0.1 ug/plate. GQD induced a cytotoxic effect in Salmonella Typhimurim TA98 at 100 and 1000 μg/plate ( Fig. 1E), after a slight ascending dose-dependent response. Although not statistically significant, GQD induced a greater number of TA100 revertant colonies at 0.1 μg/plate (Fig. 1F), and a decrease in revertants was observed at higher concentrations (1–1000 μg/plate). No mutagenicity was observed for TA104 (Fig. 1H). GQD induced a mutagenic response for TA102 (Fig. 1G). A progression can be observed as from the lowest concentration, culminating in a statistically significant difference at the highest dose (1000 μg/plate). These results are compared with a previous assay, carried out in the absence of exogenous metabolization [22]. Briefly, GQD showed a cytotoxic effect in TA98 (Fig. 1A) and TA100 (Fig. 1B) at 10 μg/plate and induced mutagenicity in TA102 at 0.1 μg/plate (Fig. 1C) and TA104 at 1 μg/plate (Fig. 1D).

Regarding the strains evaluated, mutagenicity was observed at 100 μg/plate for TA97a both in the presence and absence of exogenous metabolization ( Fig. 2A), followed by a cytotoxic effect at the highest concentration (1000 μg/plate). No effect was observed in the presence of the S9 mix (Fig. 2B); and the same was observed for TA1535 with and without the S9 mix (Fig. 2C–D).

GQD impacted the survival rate of the lines tested, particularly the 3T3BalbC cells. In the first 24 h of exposure, GQD induced low levels of cytotoxicity both in 3T3BalbC and HepG2 ( Fig. 3A and 3), as supported by the 50% lethal concentration (LC50) of > 1000 μg/mL (Fig. 3G). However, as the time of exposure increased, so did the cytotoxicity of GQD.

After 48 h of treatment, the GDQ 50% lethal concentration (LC50) for 3T3BalbC was 119.6 ± 1.8 μg/mL and 72.2 ± 10.5 μg/mL for the WST-1 and LDH assays, respectively (Fig. 3B). The LC50 was further decreased after 72 h, Fig. 3C, reaching values of 39. 9 ± 1.0 μg/mL and 10.5 ± 1.7 μg/mL for the same assays (Fig. 3G).

Meanwhile HepG2 showed higher values for LC50. After 48 h, (Fig. 3E) the LC50 for the WST-1 assay was 342.8 ± 3.5 μgmL, and 206.3 ± 1.4 μg/mL for the LDH assay. As for 3T3BalbC, HepG2 showed the lowest LC50 after 72 h, (Fig. 3F) reaching 115.8 ± 8.7 μg/mL and 85.2 ± 11.3 μg/mL for the WST-1 and LDH assays, respectively (Fig. 3G).

3T3BalbC showed a higher frequency of micronucleated cells at 10, 100 (p < 0.05) and 1000 μg/mL (p < 0.01) ( Fig. 4A) and a lower NDI at 1000 μg/mL (p < 0.01) indicating a cytostatic effect (Fig. 4B). Although no statistically significant effect was observed in the HepG2 cells, the data indicated a dose-response tendency for both the MN frequency (Fig. 4C) and NDI (Fig. 4D).

GQD only induced a mutagenic response at 100 μg/plate in the TA97a S. Typhimurium strain in the absence of exogenous metabolization (S9 mix), and at 1000 μg/plate in the TA102 strain in the presence of exogenous metabolization, as detected by the Ames test. In comparison with the authors’ previous results for the mutagenicity assessment of GQD by the Ames test without exogenous metabolization [22], one can assume a reduction in mutagenicity as detected in both TA102 and TA104 in the presence of 4% S9 mix, as observed in the present study, with no bacterial cytotoxicity. This result is further supported by the TA97a strain response, where once again mutagenicity decreased in the presence of metabolism.

Both TA102 and TA104 are recommended to assess the mutagenicity generated by oxidative stress, reinforcing the fact that this is a general concern with respect to carbon nanomaterials [4]. TA102 is competent for the nucleotide excision repair system (NER) and can detect cross-linking agents [25] as well as radiomimetic materials and single and double strand breaker agents. It is probable that GQD induces an overactivation of this system [14] or can act as a cross-linking agent.

The presence of the plasmid pKM101 is another peculiarity of TA102 [25]. This plasmid prompts translesion DNA repair, an error-prone system triggered when the DNA is severely impaired [39]. A lack of mutagenicity has been reported for a variety of carbonaceous nanomaterials [5,26,34,35,38,42,43]. Most of the assays carried out did not include TA102 or TA104, even though ROS generation is a known mechanism for nanomaterial mutagenicity [11].

Regarding the mammal cell models, 3T3Balbc, a non CYP metabolizing cell line, and HepG2, a competent CYP metabolic cell, both well-established models in toxicological evaluation, were adopted as preliminary trial models in the genotoxicity assessment [33]. The cytotoxicity detected in HepG2 and 3T3BalbC showed similar patterns. However, 3T3BalbC showed greater sensitivity to the effects of GQD, and greater damage induced by the agent in the LDH assay in comparison to WST-1 after 72 h. The release of LDH is related to the loss of membrane integrity and may indicate necrotic events [6]. Considering that most nanomaterials are known to generate membrane damage and increase ROS production [18], GQD possibly induced oxidative damage in both the intracellular microenvironment and by impairing the cell membrane.

In the present study, the dose- and time-dependent responses in the cell viability assays, showing higher levels for LDH cytotoxicity as compared to WST-1 toxicity, suggested a tendency for GQD-induced necrosis. Hence there is evident membrane damage and low mitochondrial dysfunction for each of the 3 exposure times tested, both for HepG2 and 3T3/BalbC cells. Membrane lesions can be associated with necrosis, and thus indirectly measured by LDH [3].

The micronucleus assay showed a clear genotoxic response for the 3T3/BalbC cells. In agreement with the cytotoxicity results, the micronucleus assay results suggest that the 3T3BalbC cells lose their membrane integrity and suffer nuclear damage that needs to be repaired, or, invariably, they will be submitted to necrosis. Thus, the reduction in NDI suggests that the cytostatic effects observed for the fibroblast lineage may affect the proper functioning of the cell cycle. Likewise, it has been reported that graphene oxide induced apoptosis in mouse embryonic fibroblast cells and cell cycle arrest in the S phase [15]. On the other hand, the present results for HepG2 cells did not suggest cell cycle arrest and only a slight, but non-significant dose-response tendency for genotoxicity in hepatic cells.

The presence of necrosis can favour an inflammatory and oxidative microenvironment and is commonly associated with the presence of ROS, a higher incidence of single and double strand breaks and point mutation transitions, and with chromosomal abnormalities [29]. Thus, the evidence for GQD-induced 3T3BalbC micronucleated cells corroborates the previous results of the authors, which demonstrated mutagenicity in the Ames Test in the absence of metabolic activation, with the induction of TA102 and TA104 S. enterica Typhimurium revertant colonies, which can frequently detect oxidative mutagens [22].

In the present study GQD did not exert either bacterial mutagenicity or mammal cell genotoxicity in competent CYP metabolic systems. It is possible that CYP activity on the carbonaceous nanomaterial can reduce its oxidative-related reactivity, thus reducing its capacity to interact and damage the DNA.

Some xenobiotics induce direct DNA damage, mainly because of their oxidative and/or alkylating activities, but in the presence of phase I metabolism enzymes, this mutagenicity can be modulated. It can occur due to selective scavenging of reactive species by these proteins or by the cleavage of mutagen reactive domains, a widely reported mechanism of neutralization [25]. A similar profile was recently reported by our group for two nitroimidazoles derivatives. Both induced a mutagenic response in the Ames test for the TA97 and TA102 strains, which decreased in the presence of exogenous metabolism [40]. Also, sodium azide and sodium azide-containing reagents only presented a TA100 mutagenic response in the absence of the S9 mix [8].

Graphene-induced mutagenicity was previously related to both enhancing oxidative stress and DNA adduct formation [20]. It is known that graphenes can interact with CYP isoenzymes, reducing their activity and becoming attached to these proteins, demonstrating a considerable influence on them [37].

The toxicological aspects of graphene quantum dots (GQD) were evaluated in the present study. The mutagenic, cytotoxic, and genotoxic effects of this nanomaterial in different metabolic systems indicated that competent metabolism is the key to ensure the safety of this substance for human consumption. However, the impacts of its disposal and employment in the environment must be taken into consideration since the impact on organisms with no liver metabolism may be considerable.

CFA-L, RS-O and IF supervised the project. IF, RS-O and CFA-L designed the experiments. ACG and CFA-L wrote the manuscript with contributions from all authors. Mutagenicity and Eukaryotic cell viability were determined in the cell cultures by ACG, ASF, CFA-L and IF. The CBMN assays were carried out by ACG, ASF and CFA-L. All authors critically revised the manuscript and gave their final approval.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank the financial support by the Coordination of Superior Level Staff Improvement (CAPES) (Code 0001); The Carlos Chagas Filho Foundation for Research Support of the State of Rio de Janeiro (FAPERJ), awards: E-26/200.535/2020 (ACG), E-26/202.759/2017 (IF), E26/202.256/2018 (ASF), E-26/010.002119/2019 (CFA-L and IF), E-26/010.100956/2018 (CFA-L) and E-26/010.000981/2019 (CFA-L, RS-O and IF); and The National Council for Scientific and Technological Development (CNPq), awards: 302345/2017-5 (IF).

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: ANVISA -- Agência Nacional de Vigilância Sanitária, Guia Para a Condução de Estudos Não Clínicos de Toxicologia e Segurança Farmacológica Necessários Ao Desenvolvimento de Medicamentos - Versão 2. 2013, (2013). 〈https://www.gov.br/anvisa/pt-br/centraisdeconteudo/publicacoes/medicamentos/pesquisa-clinica〉.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Toxicogenetic assessment of a pre-workout supplement: in vitro mutagenicity, cytotoxicity, genotoxicity and glutathione determination in liver cell lines and in silico ADMET approaches
Mutation Research/Genetic Toxicology and Environmental Mutagenesis

Elsevier BV. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.mrgentox.2022.503517.
